does: This paper conducts a systematic source-code audit of local LLM agent runtimes using a novel static-analysis framework called CLAWAUDIT. The authors examine the implementation layer of agents like OpenClaw and Nanobot—specifically the prompt builder, parser, tool dispatcher, skill loader, memory writer, network client, and permission gate components—to identify security vulnerabilities at the code level. They develop a five-category vulnerability taxonomy adapted from STRIDE threat modeling and create custom static-analysis rules designed to detect agent-specific vulnerability patterns, filling a gap where prior work focused on prompt injection, malicious skills, and black-box evaluation but overlooked the actual implementation details that mediate between user intent and host-level actions.
connects: This work builds on existing research characterizing risks in LLM agent systems, including work on prompt injection attacks, malicious skill injection, and marketplace safety in agent ecosystems. It extends traditional software security auditing and STRIDE threat modeling approaches to the novel domain of local LLM agent runtimes. The paper responds to a recognized but previously unexamined vulnerability in the agent safety literature: while prior work has evaluated agent behavior through black-box testing and explored model-level threats, the actual implementation layer that translates natural-language goals into system actions has remained unaudited. This work bridges the gap between general LLM safety research and practical runtime security in privileged agent software.
enables: This work enables more rigorous security evaluation of local LLM agents and establishes a foundation for systematic vulnerability discovery in agent runtimes. By identifying implementation-level weaknesses before agents become widely deployed, it supports the development of hardened agent architectures and better security practices for agent developers. The CLAWAUDIT framework and vulnerability taxonomy can be applied across different agent implementations, potentially leading to security benchmarks, best practices, and requirements for deploying agents with access to sensitive host resources. This research also points toward future work on formal verification of agent permission gates, safer design patterns for tool dispatchers, and security-by-design principles for the agent middleware layer.
